sentences.text: I have a paper that I'd like to bring to you.
Now the group which was going to Brunnlitz, the men had to go through the concentration camp Gross Rosen.
About 700 men.
And the 300 women were supposed to go to Brunnlitz.
They would have had to go through Auschwitz.
So our group went through Gross Rosen.
After we had the shower, after they cleaned us-- this was we were still standing naked without the shirts.
We were standing naked, 700 men in November.
It was below zero.
I don't know how we survived.
We were standing in front of the crematorium.
I saw the chimney, and I saw the things coming out of the chimney.
We were sure we were going to the crematorium.
Because Schindler wasn't there.
Schindler went to Czechoslovakia.
We're standing there maybe for more than an hour, maybe two hours.
And just with-- everybody was cuddled to his self.
It was freezing.
Suddenly, a man on a white horse, a German SS man comes in, and he says to the watches, the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] who was watching us.
He says, those are Schindler Juden.
Put them right away in the barrack otherwise the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] is going to be full of sick tomorrow.
Those people are going to Schindler.
They are not supposed to stay here like this.
So they gave us some kind to put on, such a long shirts.
And they put us in the barrack.
So there were no beds.
It was just plain, empty barrack.
With the barrack would maybe hold 200 people.
They squished 700 people.
We were sitting like one in the other.
Like this we were sitting.
But at least it wasn't cold because we were warm one for the other.
We stayed in Brunnlitz, I would say, about-- in Gross Rosen we stayed over a week, maybe 12 days.
But we didn't do any work.
But we did exercise the whole day.
They took us in the morning.
Exercise in the morning.
Exercise in the evening.
Exercise-- we had to run the whole camp.
We didn't know what's going to be at the end.
There was no end to it.
Suddenly one day, they said, you're going to Brunnlitz.
They put us--
Nothing.
It was watery food.
This was horrible camp, Gross Rosen.
It was an extermination camp.
And there was a lot of Polacks there who were running the camp.
And then they were horrible to us.
But we stayed together, and we were called the Schindler's group.
So they didn't touch us too much.
But food we didn't have too much.
We were very exhausted there.
And we didn't work.
Just waiting, and waiting, and waiting.
Suddenly one day, after maybe after 12 days, they told us we're going to Brunnlitz.
All right.
And again, they put us on the-- we didn't march.
They put us on the wagons again on the--
Cattle cars.
The cattle one.
And they took us.
It didn't take too long, maybe seven, eight hours traveling.
And we arrived to Brunnlitz.
The Brunnlitz was a little town in Czechoslovakia.
When we arrived at the station Schindler was there standing with his hat.
When we saw him, we saw like we-- our Savior is here.
We'd be safe now.
He said, you don't have to worry.
I'll take care of you.
And you're going to work in my factory, and you'll survive.
That's what he was telling.
We marched to the camp.
The camp was just a building.
On the first floor was the factory, where there was nothing there yet.
All the machines were not mounted.
They were waiting for us to come.
And we were supposed to be sleeping-- our quarters were supposed to be on top.
There were no beds.
There was nothing.
We were sleeping on the straw for a few days.
And then they brought some bunks.
But the bunks had only two floors.
One on the bottom, one on the top.
And it was not enough.
So the youngsters my age were sleeping under the bunk.
You know, you put in there a little mattress there from straw and you couldn't move.
You just slide in and that's how you slept for the night.
You couldn't move because if you moved, you picked up the other guy with the mattress.
So after a few weeks, they added another floor to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the bunks.
And then we got the top thing.
It was already OK.
I was working in the beginning in the also in the road.
We used to-- we made the fences with those wires they shouldn't run.
We made those for the guards, those houses they should watch us.
And then Mr.
Banker that I knew from before who put me on the list, he saw that I'm working so hard.
And he said, I'll give you a better job.
He gave me a job to work in a warehouse where we kept all the tools and all the supplies.
It was like-- it was nothing.
I was working there.
I was somebody.
And the next door it was the warehouse from food.
So there was Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH] He was running the food department.
And the warehouse was running-- [NON-ENGLISH], a German Jew.
And I was-- I got along very well with him.
So I had a very easy job there.
I can't complain on Brunnlitz.
And Schindler used to come there quite often because Banker was there often.
And we used to have a stove because that's how we kept warm there.
And the stove had two bottoms.
It was in the middle a bottom and then the top.
So we used to cook on the middle bottom there all kind of food.
And Schindler used to come and he used to ask me, what's cooking today, if he smelled it.
But the Germans didn't-- the wacht-- they couldn't do anything to us.
And when Passover came, Mr.
Banker told me he's not going to eat any bread.
I mean, there wasn't too much bread but he had enough bread.
So I cooked for him all kinds of stuff.
I cook for him [NON-ENGLISH] and potatoes.
And he was-- he and there was a rabbi, Levertov, who is in the book, too.
In Schindler's list, original list when he-- and I was helping him to eat Passover.
And I already stole a little bit for myself, too.
So I don't have to buy.
We even have-- they baked two, three matzohs.
And we had the electric ovens.
So people baked some matzoh.
Everybody got a little piece, you know, to make a bracha when Pesach came.
And in the camp, in Brunnlitz, there was the commander.
His name was-- I have it written down on a piece of paper.
Sometimes I forget already.
And he was a Nazi.
He was sent from [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] from a big camp.
Leopold was his name.
And he couldn't do anything because Schindler was there.
Once Schindler wasn't there, he showed his what he could do.
In the winter, it was in January-- we came there in like November.
It was January, February, it was already by the end of the war.
Beg your pardon?
There was a camp [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And they liquidated the camp.
They put about 100 Jews in a wagon, and no camp wanted to take them in.
They went to Auschwitz, and they went to this camp.
Nobody wanted to take them in.
And they were riding for two weeks in the cold weather.
Half-frozen people.
Nobody-- no camp wanted to take them came in.
Somebody called Schindler from [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which was the next town.
And they told them, there is a wagon here with Jews where they hear some noises from them.
Nobody wants to take them.
He says, send them into Brunnlitz.
We had a place where the trains would come in.
I wasn't there when they opened the door, but a friend of mine was there.
It was a horrible thing.
People were half-frozen.
Naked, frozen people with all kinds of shapes.
You couldn't take them out because their skin was frozen to the floor from the wagon.
You have to put hot water to get them out.
A friend of mine who lives here, his name is Mr.
Heller, he was a strong guy, and he went to help the people out.
I saw the people when they brought them to the rooms there.
They there-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] skeletons and they were half alive.
They were just frozen.
Your limbs, you know, you couldn't do nothing.
And some of them survived.
Some of them.
And then Mrs.
Schindler, Schindler's wife, she used to bring them all kind of food to nourish them.
But this was a horrible scene.
And that Leopold, the commander, wanted to dispose of them.
Wanted to burn them.
There should be no sign left.
Schindler didn't let him.
He bought a piece of cemetery in the town, and they had a Jewish burial.
We said Kaddish for them and everything.
Unbelievable.
And he was always-- Schindler when everything happened something he was always there.
And then and once Goeth came to him.
You know, Goeth was put to jail for all this black market things what he did in Plaszow.
He took so much money from the Jewish people.
And he was buying all kind of gold and silver.
So he came back.
He had nothing.
People were making jokes of him, the prisoners, our people.
He couldn't do anything anymore.
Because he wasn't in charge anymore.
Schindler was in charge.
Schindler used to take his truck and used to go to organize food.
Wherever they had some factories he used to bring in loaves of bread to us, all kind of food.
He just went in the morning with the Jewish affair.
He knew where to go.
And he brought all kind of stuff we should have enough food to eat.
And people who survived Schindler, you have my picture there, when I was there they all looked pretty well.
Not like the Muselmanns from other camps.
A Muselmann is a person who is a skeleton.

And if you became a Muselmann if you didn't take care of yourself, if you didn't wash, and you didn't care for yourself, you didn't do anything, then you become officially a Muselmann, and it was the end of you.
Because you didn't take care of your-- you became I would say, resigned from life.
You didn't care what happens to you.
There was-- I had a friend in Plaszow where there was an [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], you know, they took him to another camp to a crematoriums.
They gave whoever wanted to go you got a loaf of bread.
A loaf of bread, this was like a million dollars.
So he said, I'm going to die anyhow.
Let me get a loaf of bread.
He took a loaf of bread, and he ate it up, and they took him to one of the big camps.
And he was-- he went to the crematorium.
But this was a Muselmann was.
He didn't care.
He let himself go.
And if you-- and they saw if you were like this, it was finished.
We tried to stay as much as we can.
Well, we didn't know too much.
But we heard from, you know, people come in.
All kind of news that the Russians are coming closer and closer.
Poland was already half liberated by Russians.
And I heard a story that Leopold, the commander, had orders to liquidate our camp.
He had the Czechs made graves in a forest not far from our camp, from Brunnlitz.
And when he gets closer, he's going to shoot us all.
Schindler found out about it, that he got some kind of orders to liquidate the camp.
He had so much pull in Berlin.
And he took him and they sent him to the front, to the Russian front-- Leopold.
And we had no more commander.
We only had the watchmens.
They were Germans, but they were mostly Wehrmacht people but not SS men.
And Schindler was the head of the camp.
So he was telling us what's going on.
SS got closer and closer.
OK.
When it was getting closer, we felt something is getting closer.
Schindler said, that he's not going to stay here.
He's going to go to on the American side because he's afraid to be caught by the Russians.
It was the Russians can do a lot of things.
What he did all the time he organized all kinds of things for us.
He went to this factory.
He took out materials.
He went to a factory to get leather.
He took out the factory took out cameras.
And everything was laying in a warehouse sealed.
Nobody went in there.
He put everything in the warehouse.
When it came to the end of the-- before the liberation, he called us to-- we went to a big room in the factory.
And he told us that the war is coming to an end.
People should not go out to the cities and kill people to take revenge.
He says, he prepared here a warehouse of stuff.
Everybody is going to get his part, so he will have what to start our life with.
And he had a speech for us.
And we heard the speech from Churchill when he was talking about the end of the war, that the war is ending.
They signing the thing.
And we were rejoicing.
And you saw the movie, you saw the Schindler's farewell speech.
He was telling us that we are going to be liberated in a day or two and we should not take revenge on the people, as I said before.
That we should go slowly back to our normal life.
And you saw there was a band.
We wanted to give Schindler something for his job, but we had nothing.
There was an old man.
His name was [NON-ENGLISH].
He was from Krakow.
And he had a gold bridge on his teeth, which they didn't took it out.
Because the Germans took out all the gold bridges and all the caps from the-- he says, he wants to give this to make something for Schindler.
And there was one man who was very handy.
And he made a ring out of it.
[SOBBING] And I witness it when they gave it to him.
He cried.
And he said, he's leaving now.
He's going away.
He doesn't know if he will see us again.
But he-- and one of the drivers, the Jewish drivers-- I think about three or four went with him.
And they put all.
He had a lot of diamonds, a lot of gold.
So they put it in the cars, you know, in the walls.
Whatever he had, he deserved it.
Because he saved about 1,100 people from death.
Whatever people say he was this and this, I would never let say any bad word on him.
Because if not for him, I wouldn't be here.
And my family wouldn't be here.
So we were.
And he left.
And we were for two days by ourselves.
No Germans.
But the Germans run away and our boys took guns and stayed in the front of the doors of camp.
And I remember they came in and German convoy came in with a jeep, about six of them with guns.
And they wanted into the camp.
So the boy stayed with the guns like this.
He said, this is a private camp.
You cannot come in.
I've seen this myself.
And they walked away.
And one day, maybe two days later, we were sitting around.
You know, we couldn't go out yet.
And a Russian soldier comes on a horse.
And he says, you are liberated.
We are the Russians.
And you know, people started to kiss him, and hug him, and carry him.
As a matter of fact, he was a Jewish guy.
But he didn't know too much about the Judaism.
And I remember we took-- in one of the in the factory we took out some stuff.
We put a table, and we had some white tablecloths.
We put in on the tablecloths.
We put a loaf of bread.
And we while we were standing around, he was sitting there with the people.
And we were singing, you know.
And the Germans, the watchmen who were watching us run away.
So our people started to go to town already.
And they found that one of the watchmen who was a very bad guy in civilian clothes already.
They brought him to camp, and they hanged him on a machine from you know, from the top.
And I have never seen something like this.
People were coming and pulling things off of him.
He said, this is for my mother, this is for my father.
It was such a revenge I have never seen in my life.
But he was hanged.
He died.
They took him out later.
But then it started, I remember.
I have a friend who lives in San Francisco.
His name is Henry Slamowicz.
And we were most together because we kept together.
As a matter of fact, he looked like me and I looked like him.
A lot of people made a mistake.
He told this to me.
And after that, we burst out crying.
Because where are we going to go?
I mean, we were left alone.
I mean, he was my age, and we were crying.
A lot of people started to leave Brunnlitz.
They went by train.
They went by horse and buggy, you know.
And after three, four days I went-- I had another friend.
His name was Julek Waserlaug.
He was from [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], the other town.
And he was-- I was also going because that Slamowicz is from Krakow.
So we said, I'll go with him, because we go to our hometown.
Maybe somebody is alive still.
So we took the train.
And we were going with the train.
And the Russians, not once they wanted to steal our stuff.
They wanted to take away our stuff from us.
There was that liberate us.
And we came to Krakow, because this was the main-- everybody came to Krakow.
And we stayed in the Krakow.
They had a Jewish [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] there.
Like Jewish committee who all the refugees, all the camps, people from camp came there.
And we slept there for two, three days.
And then we decided we're going to go to [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] to my hometown and his hometown.
We went by train.
And I came to my hometown.
There was nobody there.
And not a sign of a Jew.
Our house was occupied already by Christians.
And the shul was in a shambles, you know.
The Germans they put horses there.
There was nothing to look there for.
I walked around in the streets.
I walked around here.
And I stayed in [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] maybe.
And then I heard that the Jews are being killed in Kielce.
There was a pogrom.
They killed about-- a number of Jews who came back from camps.
The Pollack.
And there was the army-- AK, they called it.
The army of [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
Polish soldiers were killing Jews.
And I had a bad experience.
I came into my hometown, and a boy was there from school.
He says, you're still alive?
Instead of telling me, oh, I'm glad to see you.
He says, you're still alive?
They didn't kill you?
So I had no feeling to stay in the town.
First of all, I was afraid to sleep there by myself.
One Jew in the town.
The Pollacks were very bad at that time.
So I went to [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] and we stayed there.
And then we saw there is no future.
So we like burned all the bridges and went back to Krakow.
And in Krakow we stayed for a while.
And then we joined a group who was going to Israel, like a kibbutz.
And we went back to Czechoslovakia.
We smuggled the border.
And we had some-- my friend found something from his hometown, some bracelets from his mother.
So we had to-- on the border they were checking us.
We smuggled the border but sometimes we were caught.
So we went to Czechoslovakia, went to Bratislava.
There was a whole group about 20 people.
Some of them we're still in touch with.
And one lives in Brazil, one lives in New Jersey, one lives in-- but we're still in touch with them.
Sometimes we meet in Florida.
We kept very close.
It was like a whole family.
There were like eight people.
But we were kept very close with each other.
And from Bratislava we went to Hungary, Budapest.
And we stayed there in a school.
And the HIAS, the Jewish committee used to help us.
So we were going to Israel.
So we had to go somewhere where there is and ocean to board a boat.
So we had to go to Italy.
We went to the border also on black-- it was a funny thing.
They told us to go in the wagons.
Like unfortunately, we were with the Germans.
There was in Ital-- and we went from Austria at that time.
From Austria there were the British.
And the British didn't want you to go to Israel.
They didn't want to let anybody into Israel because they occupied Israel at that time.
It was the mandate.
And we had to go to Italy.
And then didn't let the Jews go.
So at night, we had to go into-- when they didn't see, we were going to the wagons, laid down straight.
And they used to come with the flashlights but didn't see.
They never looked on the bottom, so.
But what happened one night we went, and they forgot to attach our wagon to the other train.
So the train went away and we stayed there.
But so we went the next night we went through.
When we came to Italy it was like we come to paradise.
Beautiful country and the people were very nice to us.
Really nice.
Then I went to-- I was in camp Cremona.
I came in 1945.
DP camp, yeah.
And there were about, I would say, about 1,500 people in that camp.
And everybody was there was going to Israel.
Because this was the illegal boats.
And what happened, they used to catch the boats, and they put the people to Cyprus.
Behind wires again in another camp.
I said, no, I'm not going.
My friends, we decided we are not coming.
We would wait until we could go legal.
So we decided not to go to Israel because we didn't want to be in the camp again.
We just came out of the camp.
So we stayed in Italy.
I was there for 4 and 1/2 years.
And I was young, and I was not attached.
And I had a very good time there.
I was free.
I used to travel all over.
And we saw every city.
And I had a job there, too, in Italy.
And when I was in Poland, I went into my Goyish neighbor.
I used to play with her son.
And he wasn't there.
The son was on a different town.
And I told her, look, anybody comes from my family, they're probably going to write to you.
Because you were the closest neighbors.
I'm going to write to you wherever I'll be.
Maybe somebody will come up.
We were eight children, and there were the grandchildren, and all this.
Maybe somebody is alive.
You felt like you were all alone.
So when I was in Italy in that camp, I wrote to them.
I even sent them some packages because I wanted to attach to them.
Especially they were from my hometown.
Even they were Goyim, but I felt so much closeness to the town.
So I used to write to them.
One day, I get a postcard, and it's from my sister.
My sister was in Russia.
And they came later than we came to Poland.
When she came to Poland, she wrote to that family, to that Christian family.
And the Christian family gave her my address in Italy.
My older sister.
This was the only one who survived.
She was there with her husband and two, three children.
One child died there.
They were in Siberia, yeah.
So I got the postcard from her.
It's like I got a new way, a thing of life.
I have a sister.
I'm not alone yet.
So it was like, you know, we wrote letters, love letters back and forth, back and forth.
I used to send her packages from Italy when she lived in Poland there.
And it was hard for them.
I was alone, and I would manage some way.
And my wish was to see her.
I mean, from all those years I never saw her before.
I mean, if I would meet in the street, I wouldn't recognize her.
And--
